review.method: Item-by-item review of stored official question wording, primary answer chain and imported mark-scheme rows against the local official AQA curriculum; no prompt classifier or model call.
review.boundary: Only a deterministic learner-selectable Physics chapter is mapped. Pure graph, scale, percentage, uncertainty or proportionality work with incidental science context is explicitly withheld.

question.promptText: Explain why the total positive charge in every atom of an element is always the same.
question.selfContainedPromptText: Explain why the total positive charge in every atom of an element is always the same.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Proton charge
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: positive charge -> protons -> same element -> same protons -> same charge

markSchemeItems.text: Positive charge is provided by protons.
Every atom of the same element contains the same number of protons.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 6.4 Atomic structure.

question.promptText: Plan an experiment that would allow the student to determine the density of the object.
question.selfContainedPromptText: A student wanted to determine the density of the irregular shaped object shown in Figure 3. Plan an experiment that would allow the student to determine the density of the object.
question.contextText: A student wanted to determine the density of the irregular shaped object shown in Figure 3.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Displacement Density
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: balance mass -> water displacement -> volume difference -> mass ÷ volume

markSchemeItems.text: Level 3: The method would lead to the production of a valid outcome. All key steps are identified and logically sequenced.
Level 2: The method would not necessarily lead to a valid outcome. Most steps are identified, but the method is not fully logically sequenced.
Level 1: The method would not lead to a valid outcome. Some relevant steps are identified, but links are not made clear.
Measure mass using a top pan balance or scales.
Find volume by water displacement in a measuring cylinder: record initial volume, place object in water, record final volume, and calculate final volume minus initial volume.
Alternatively, use a displacement/eureka can: fill to the spout, place object in water, collect displaced water, and measure its volume with a measuring cylinder.
Calculate density using $\text{density}=\frac{\text{mass}}{\text{volume}}$.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 6.3 Particle model of matter.

question.promptText:
Which **two** sentences describe the movement of the air particles in the canister?

Tick **two** boxes.
question.selfContainedPromptText: Figure 5 shows a diver using a canister of compressed air so that he can breathe underwater. Which two sentences describe the movement of the air particles in the canister? Tick two boxes.
question.contextText: Figure 5 shows a diver. The diver is using a canister of compressed air so that he can breathe underwater.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Gas Motion
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: gas particles -> random directions -> range of speeds

markSchemeItems.text: They move in random directions.
They move with a range of different speeds.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 6.3 Particle model of matter.

question.promptText:
The temperature of the air inside the canister increases.

What happens to the movement of the air particles?
question.selfContainedPromptText: The temperature of the air inside the canister increases. What happens to the movement of the air particles?

primaryAnswerChain.title: Gas Pressure
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: temperature/density -> particle motion -> wall collisions -> pressure change
primaryAnswerChain.fitNotes: Temperature increase means gas particles move faster.

markSchemeItems.text: The mean speed of the particles would increase.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 6.3 Particle model of matter.

question.promptText:
It could be dangerous if the temperature of the air inside the canister increased by a large amount.

Explain why.
question.selfContainedPromptText: It could be dangerous if the temperature of the air inside the canister increased by a large amount. Explain why.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Gas Pressure
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: temperature/density -> particle motion -> wall collisions -> pressure change
primaryAnswerChain.fitNotes: Temperature increase causes pressure increase, which can make the canister explode.

markSchemeItems.text: If the temperature increases, the pressure increases.
The canister could explode.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 6.3 Particle model of matter.

question.promptText: What happens to the volume of the air when it is released from the canister?
question.selfContainedPromptText: What happens to the volume of the air when it is released from the canister?

primaryAnswerChain.title: Gas expands
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: compressed air -> released -> volume increases

markSchemeItems.text: The volume of the air increases.

question.promptText:
What is an alpha particle?

Tick **one** box.
question.selfContainedPromptText: The Chernobyl disaster released radioactive isotopes that emitted alpha, beta and gamma radiation. What is an alpha particle? Tick one box.
question.contextText: The Chernobyl disaster was a nuclear accident that happened in 1986. Radioactive isotopes were released into the environment. The radioactive isotopes emitted alpha, beta and gamma radiation.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Alpha Makeup
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: alpha particle -> helium nucleus -> 2 protons -> 2 neutrons

markSchemeItems.text: An alpha particle contains 2 charged particles and 2 neutral particles.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 6.4 Atomic structure.

question.selfContainedPromptText: Which statement about beta radiation is true? Tick one box.
question.contextText: The radioactive isotopes emitted alpha, beta and gamma radiation.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Beta Charge
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: beta radiation -> negative charge

markSchemeItems.text: Beta radiation is the type of radiation with a negative charge.

Which statement about gamma radiation is true?

Tick **one** box.

question.contextText: The radioactive isotopes emitted alpha, beta and gamma radiation.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Gamma Range

markSchemeItems.text: Gamma radiation has a very long range in air.

question.promptText:
A soil sample was taken from the area around Chernobyl in 1986

The soil sample was contaminated with equal amounts of caesium–137 and iodine–131

Explain how the risk linked to each isotope has changed between 1986 and 2018

Both isotopes emit the same type of radiation.
question.selfContainedPromptText: Table 3 shows half-lives: caesium–137 has a half-life of 30 years and iodine–131 has a half-life of 8 days. A soil sample was taken from the area around Chernobyl in 1986. The soil sample was contaminated with equal amounts of caesium–137 and iodine–131. Explain how the risk linked to each isotope has changed between 1986 and 2018. Both isotopes emit the same type of radiation.
question.contextText: Table 3 shows the half-lives of two of the radioactive isotopes that contaminated the environment.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Radiation risk
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: radiation type -> dose/activity -> shield/decay -> risk change
primaryAnswerChain.fitNotes: Compare elapsed time with each half-life to explain how risk changes.

markSchemeItems.text: The risk or activity associated with iodine-131 has decreased by a large amount.
This is because iodine-131 has a short half-life.
The risk or activity associated with caesium-137 will not have decreased by much.
This is because caesium-137 has a long half-life.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 6.4 Atomic structure.

question.promptText: Determine the year when the activity of the caesium–137 in the soil sample will be 1/32 of its original value.
question.selfContainedPromptText: Caesium–137 has a half-life of 30 years. The soil sample was taken in 1986. Determine the year when the activity of the caesium–137 in the soil sample will be $\frac{1}{32}$ of its original value.
question.contextText: Table 3 shows caesium–137 has a half-life of 30 years.

markSchemeItems.text: Recognise that $\frac{1}{32}$ is reached after 5 half-lives.
Calculate elapsed time: $5\times30=150$ years.
Calculate the year: $1986+150=2136$.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 6.4 Atomic structure.

question.promptText: Explain how the wall reduces unwanted energy transfers.
question.selfContainedPromptText: Figure 7 shows cavity wall insulation being installed in the wall of a house. Explain how the wall reduces unwanted energy transfers.
question.contextText: Figure 7 shows cavity wall insulation being installed in the wall of a house.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Conductivity rate
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: conductivity level -> transfer rate -> energy direction -> design purpose
primaryAnswerChain.fitNotes: Low thermal conductivity and multiple layers reduce conduction through the wall.

markSchemeItems.text: The wall has two or three layers, or is thick.
The cavity wall insulation, brick or block has a low thermal conductivity.
Less energy is transferred by conduction, or the rate of energy transfer is lower.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 6.1 Energy.

question.promptText:
**Figure 8** shows the gas boiler used to heat the house.

Describe how different energy stores are changed by the boiler.
question.selfContainedPromptText: Figure 8 shows the gas boiler used to heat the house. Describe how different energy stores are changed by the boiler.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Heating Stores
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: fuel chemical down -> receiver thermal up -> each receiver
primaryAnswerChain.fitNotes: Chemical store of fuel decreases; thermal stores of water and air increase.

markSchemeItems.text: The chemical energy store of the fuel decreases.
The thermal energy store of the water increases.
The thermal energy store of the air or atmosphere increases.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 6.1 Energy.

question.promptText:
To heat the house, the boiler transfers 15 MJ of energy in 10 minutes.

Calculate the power of the boiler.

Write any equation that you use.
question.selfContainedPromptText: To heat the house, the boiler transfers 15 MJ of energy in 10 minutes. Calculate the power of the boiler. Write any equation that you use.

markSchemeItems.text: Convert energy: $E=15\,000\,000\ \mathrm{J}$.
Convert time: $t=600\ \mathrm{s}$.
Use $P=\frac{E}{t}$ with the substituted values, e.g. $P=\frac{15\,000\,000}{600}$.
Power is $25\,000\ \mathrm{W}$.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 6.1 Energy.

question.promptText: Sketch a current potential difference graph for a filament lamp on **Figure 9**
question.selfContainedPromptText: A student built a circuit using filament lamps. Sketch a current-potential difference graph for a filament lamp on Figure 9.
question.contextText: A student built a circuit using filament lamps.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Lamp I-V
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: origin curve -> first and third -> decreasing gradient

markSchemeItems.text: A curve in the first and third quadrants only, passing through the origin.
The curve has decreasing gradient as potential difference increases in magnitude.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 6.2 Electricity.

question.promptText: Compare the currents I$_1$, I$_2$ and I$_3$
question.selfContainedPromptText: Figure 10 shows a circuit with two identical filament lamps in parallel. Compare the currents $I_1$, $I_2$ and $I_3$.
question.contextText: Figure 10 shows the circuit with two identical filament lamps.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Parallel Currents
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: parallel branches -> same p.d. -> equal branches -> currents add
primaryAnswerChain.fitNotes: Identical branches mean equal branch currents; total current is their sum.

markSchemeItems.text: Any two correct comparisons: $I_1=I_2+I_3$; $I_2=I_3$; $I_1=2I_2$; $I_1=2I_3$.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 6.2 Electricity.

question.promptText:
Calculate the charge that flows through the cell in 1 minute.

Each filament lamp has a power of 3 W and a resistance of 12 Ω

Write any equations that you use.

Give the unit.
question.selfContainedPromptText: In the circuit with two identical filament lamps in parallel, calculate the charge that flows through the cell in 1 minute. Each filament lamp has a power of 3 W and a resistance of 12 Ω. Write any equations that you use. Give the unit.
question.contextText: Use the circuit from Figure 10: two identical filament lamps in parallel connected to a cell.

markSchemeItems.text: Use $P=I^2R$ for one lamp: $3=I^2\times12$.
Rearrange to $I=\sqrt{\frac{3}{12}}$.
Current in one lamp is $0.5\ \mathrm{A}$.
Use total current through the cell as $1.0\ \mathrm{A}$, or calculate charge for one branch $Q=0.5\times60=30\ \mathrm{C}$.
Total charge through the cell is $60$.
Unit is coulombs or C.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 6.2 Electricity.

question.promptText:
The student builds a different circuit.

**Figure 11** shows the circuit.

Explain how the readings on both meters change when the environmental conditions change.
question.selfContainedPromptText: The student builds a different circuit. Figure 11 shows a circuit with a fixed resistor in series with an LDR, an ammeter in series and a voltmeter across the LDR. Explain how the readings on both meters change when the environmental conditions change.

primaryAnswerChain.title: LDR divider
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: light level -> LDR resistance -> circuit current -> voltage share
primaryAnswerChain.fitNotes: More light lowers LDR resistance, raises current, and lowers the LDR voltage share.

markSchemeItems.text: Level 3: Relevant points are identified, given in detail and logically linked to form a clear account.
Level 2: Relevant points are identified, and there are attempts at logically linking. The resulting account is not fully clear.
Level 1: Points are identified and stated simply, but their relevance is not clear and there is no attempt at logical linking.
The resistance of the LDR changes when light intensity changes.
When light intensity increases, the resistance of the LDR decreases.
The overall resistance of the circuit decreases.
The potential difference across the total resistance remains unchanged.
The current in the ammeter increases.
The potential difference across the fixed resistor increases.
The potential difference across the LDR decreases.
The reading on the voltmeter decreases.
Potential difference is shared between the components in series; the lower the resistance of the LDR, the smaller its share of the potential difference.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 6.2 Electricity.

question.promptText: Suggest why the line on **Figure 5** does not go through the origin.
question.selfContainedPromptText: Figure 5 shows how the mass of water in the kettle affected the time taken for the kettle to switch off. Suggest why the line on Figure 5 does not go through the origin.
question.contextText: Figure 5 shows how the mass of water in the kettle affected the time taken for the kettle to switch off.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Thermal Intercept
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: apparatus thermal mass -> heats first -> positive intercept

markSchemeItems.text: The heating element, or the kettle, took time to heat up.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 6.1 Energy.

question.promptText: Suggest why the results give a non-linear pattern.
question.selfContainedPromptText: Figure 5 shows how the mass of water in the kettle affected the time taken for the kettle to switch off. Suggest why the results give a non-linear pattern.
question.contextText: Figure 5 shows how the mass of water in the kettle affected the time taken for the kettle to switch off.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Kettle efficiency
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: larger water mass -> lower loss per kg -> higher efficiency -> non-linear heating
primaryAnswerChain.fitNotes: Bigger loads waste less per kilogram.

markSchemeItems.text: The rate of energy transfer per kg of water to the surroundings decreases as the mass of water increases; or the efficiency of the kettle increases as the mass of water increases.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 6.1 Energy.

question.promptText:
The power of the kettle was 2.6 kW

The kettle took 120 seconds to heat 0.80 kg of water from 18 °C to 100 °C

Calculate the specific heat capacity of water using this information.

Give your answer to 2 significant figures.
question.selfContainedPromptText: The power of the kettle was 2.6 kW. The kettle took 120 seconds to heat 0.80 kg of water from 18 °C to 100 °C. Calculate the specific heat capacity of water using this information. Give your answer to 2 significant figures.

markSchemeItems.text: Use energy transferred from power and time: $E = 2600 \times 120$.
Calculate $E = 312\,000\ \mathrm{J}$.
Use the specific heat capacity equation with the temperature change: $312\,000 = 0.80 \times c \times (100-18)$, or $312\,000 = 0.80 \times c \times 82$.
Rearrange to $c = \frac{312\,000}{0.80 \times 82}$.
Calculate $c = 4756\ \mathrm{J/kg\ ^\circ C}$.
Round correctly to 2 significant figures: $c = 4800\ \mathrm{J/kg\ ^\circ C}$.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 6.3 Particle model of matter.

question.selfContainedPromptText: Lanthanum-140 is a radioactive isotope. A nucleus of lanthanum-140 emits gamma radiation. What happens to the mass number and the charge of the nucleus when gamma radiation is emitted? Tick (✓) one box.
question.contextText: Lanthanum-140 is a radioactive isotope.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Gamma Unchanged
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: gamma emission -> no nucleons lost -> mass unchanged -> charge unchanged

markSchemeItems.text: Mass number stays the same, charge stays the same.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 6.4 Atomic structure.

question.promptText: Why is it difficult to detect gamma radiation?
question.selfContainedPromptText: Lanthanum-140 is a radioactive isotope. Why is it difficult to detect gamma radiation?
question.contextText: Lanthanum-140 is a radioactive isotope.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Gamma Detection
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: gamma radiation -> weak ionisation -> passes detector

markSchemeItems.text: Gamma radiation is only weakly ionising; or most gamma radiation will pass through any detector.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 6.4 Atomic structure.

question.promptText:
Activity is the rate at which a radioactive source decays.

A teacher measured the count-rate from a sample of lanthanum-140 using a Geiger-Muller (G-M) tube.

Explain why the count rate was less than the activity of the sample of lanthanum-140.
question.selfContainedPromptText: Lanthanum-140 is a radioactive isotope. Activity is the rate at which a radioactive source decays. A teacher measured the count-rate from a sample of lanthanum-140 using a Geiger-Muller (G-M) tube. Explain why the count rate was less than the activity of the sample of lanthanum-140.
question.contextText: Lanthanum-140 is a radioactive isotope. Activity is the rate at which a radioactive source decays.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Detected Fraction
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: total decays -> emitted radiation -> missed radiation -> lower count-rate
primaryAnswerChain.fitNotes: Activity is total decays; count rate is only the detected fraction.

markSchemeItems.text: The radiation spreads out in all directions.
Only some of the radiation goes into the G-M tube.
Only some of the radiation passing into the G-M tube is detected.
Alternative full-credit linked idea: only some radiation passing into the G-M tube is detected because gamma is weakly ionising.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 6.4 Atomic structure.

question.promptText: Explain why the teacher stood as far away from the apparatus as possible.
question.selfContainedPromptText: A teacher investigated how the thickness of lead affected the amount of gamma radiation that could pass through it. Figure 6 shows a sample of lanthanum-140, lead, and a G-M tube connected to a counting machine. Explain why the teacher stood as far away from the apparatus as possible.
question.contextText: The teacher investigated how the thickness of lead affected the amount of gamma radiation that could pass through it. Figure 6 shows the apparatus.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Radiation risk
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: radiation type -> dose/activity -> shield/decay -> risk change
primaryAnswerChain.fitNotes: More distance means less radiation received and therefore lower health risk.

markSchemeItems.text: To reduce the amount of radiation received.
Because radiation increases the risk of cancer or genetic mutation.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 6.4 Atomic structure.

Lanthanum-140 can also emit beta radiation and change into cerium.

question.selfContainedPromptText: Lanthanum-140 can also emit beta radiation and change into cerium. Complete the equation showing the decay of lanthanum (La) 140 into cerium (Ce): $_{57}^{140}\mathrm{La}\rightarrow\ _{\square}^{\square}\mathrm{e}+\ _{\square}^{\square}\mathrm{Ce}$.
question.contextText: Lanthanum-140 can also emit beta radiation and change into cerium.

markSchemeItems.text: Correct numbers on the electron: mass number 0 and atomic number -1.
Correct numbers on cerium: mass number 140 and atomic number 58.

question.promptText:
The results from the alpha particle scattering experiment led to the nuclear model.

Alpha particles were fired at a thin film of gold at a speed of 7% of the speed of light.

Determine the speed of the alpha particles.

Speed of light = 300 000 000 m/s
question.selfContainedPromptText: The results from the alpha particle scattering experiment led to the nuclear model. Alpha particles were fired at a thin film of gold at a speed of 7% of the speed of light. Determine the speed of the alpha particles. Speed of light = 300 000 000 m/s.

markSchemeItems.text: Calculate 7% of the speed of light: $300\,000\,000 \times \frac{7}{100}$.
Speed is $21\,000\,000\ \mathrm{m/s}$, or $2.1\times10^7\ \mathrm{m/s}$.

withheldQuestions.reason: Official prompt and mark evidence assess a generic mathematical or working-scientifically operation; the surrounding context alone is not enough to assign one Physics curriculum chapter.

question.promptText:

A hydrogen atom has a radius of $2.5\times10^{-11}$ m

Determine the radius of a magnesium atom.

question.selfContainedPromptText: Figure 2 shows two atoms represented as solid spheres. A hydrogen atom has a radius of $2.5\times10^{-11}$ m. Determine the radius of a magnesium atom. Use measurements from Figure 2.

markSchemeItems.text: Use a radius ratio of about 6 from the measurements and substitute: $r=6\times2.5\times10^{-11}$.
Radius is in the range $1.4\times10^{-10}$ m to $1.6\times10^{-10}$ m, typically $1.5\times10^{-10}$ m.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Half Range
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: highest-lowest -> range -> halve -> ± unit
primaryAnswerChain.fitNotes: Uncertainty from repeated results is half the range.

markSchemeItems.text: Use half the range: $\frac{1120-960}{2}$.
Uncertainty is $80\ \mathrm{kg/m^3}$.

primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: pressure graph -> final plateau -> atmospheric pressure

The cavity insulation was tested.

- The temperature inside the house was measured every 20 minutes for 2 hours.

**Table 4** shows the results.

question.selfContainedPromptText: The cavity insulation was tested. The heating inside the house was switched off and the temperature inside the house was measured every 20 minutes for 2 hours. Table 4 shows the results. Determine the temperature inside the house after 30 minutes.

markSchemeItems.text: Interpolate halfway between 20.8 °C at 20 minutes and 17.4 °C at 40 minutes, for example $17.4+\frac{20.8-17.4}{2}$.

question.promptText:
The teacher concluded that the count rate was **not** inversely proportional to the thickness of lead.

Explain why the teacher was correct.

Use the data in **Table 1**.
question.selfContainedPromptText: A teacher investigated how the thickness of lead affected the amount of gamma radiation that could pass through it. Table 1 shows that as the thickness of lead increases from 0.5 cm to 2.5 cm, the count rate decreases from 110 counts per second to 10 counts per second. The teacher concluded that the count rate was not inversely proportional to the thickness of lead. Explain why the teacher was correct. Use the data in Table 1.
question.contextText: The teacher investigated how the thickness of lead affected the amount of gamma radiation that could pass through it. Table 1 shows the results.

markSchemeItems.text: Calculate a product of thickness and count rate.
Calculate a second product of thickness and count rate.
Compare the calculated products and recognise that they are different.
Alternative method: calculate half the count rate, compare with the count rate for double that thickness, and recognise that the values are different.
